caveats: a: The only usable BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541800, which includes creative, PR, and media-representative businesses outside this lens.
a: Exposure is a task judgment, not an observed automation rate.
rho: No source measures implemented AI labor savings for the frozen six-digit lens.
rho: Implementation rates will differ sharply between programmatic specialists and relationship-led traditional-media buyers.
e: The NAICS definition identifies activity, not EBITDA, ownership independence, customer concentration, or transferability.
e: Marketplace data omit many privately negotiated sales and cannot establish eligibility for all firms.
s5: S8 uses a nonrandom convenience sample that is weighted to small employer firms and is not industry-specific.
s5: S9's transactions are voluntarily reported and do not distinguish control transfers, LMM EBITDA, or the NAICS code.
q: ANA survey respondents are marketer members and may not represent LMM agencies.
q: The sources describe compensation practices, not the discounted fraction of an AI-generated gross benefit retained by an operator.
d5: Digital ad revenue is nominal spend and includes inventory purchased without an external agency.
d5: National occupational projections are not a forecast for NAICS 541830 or a five-year constant-quality service basket.
o: The cited programmatic figures are from a 2019 ANA report and its surveyed advertisers are not a census of LMM customers.
o: No source directly measures the five-year operator-required share for the frozen lens.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers buy recurring campaign-management and media-buying services through fees, retainers, project scopes, commissions, or combinations; media spend itself is typically a client pass-through rather than agency revenue retained as operating value.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive advertiser buying teams, media-owner sales representatives, full-service creative agencies whose buying is incidental, shell entities, and financing vehicles are excluded.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market agencies that repeatedly buy or manage advertising time or space for external advertiser or agency customers, including planning, trafficking, optimization, reporting, and negotiated media purchasing.

a.bridge: S2 measures the broader NAICS 541800 workforce rather than six-digit media buyers. I use its occupation and wage mix to judge the wage-weighted task exposure of 541830, placing more weight on its directly relevant market-research, project-management, purchasing, sales, and administrative work and less on creative-production roles; this likely overstates exposure if six-digit work is unusually negotiation- and relationship-intensive, and understates it if routine trafficking and reporting dominate.
caveats: The only usable BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541800, which includes creative, PR, and media-representative businesses outside this lens.
Exposure is a task judgment, not an observed automation rate.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage cross-tab, time-and-motion studies from independent media agencies, and anonymized workflow logs separating trafficking, reporting, optimization, negotiation, and account-management hours.
a.rationale: The broader advertising-services workforce has 16.77% management, 16.65% business and financial operations, 24.48% arts/design/media, 13.28% sales, and 11.22% office support. Market research analysts and marketing specialists alone are 8.15% of that workforce. Generative analysis, reporting, planning support, QA, documentation, and administrative work are materially exposed, while client stewardship, media negotiations, accountability, and judgment over brand suitability limit substitution.

d5.bridge: S7 measures nominal U.S. internet advertising revenue, while S3 and S4 measure national occupation employment projections, rather than constant-price, constant-quality quantity demand for the current 541830 service basket. I use the strong current digital-ad-spend signal and 6–7% decade occupational growth indications as directional evidence, then discount for price inflation, media-mix shifts, platform disintermediation, and the fact that spend growth need not produce proportional outsourced buying demand; this can overstate demand if spend moves in-house and understate it if fragmented media expands agency need.
caveats: Digital ad revenue is nominal spend and includes inventory purchased without an external agency.
National occupational projections are not a forecast for NAICS 541830 or a five-year constant-quality service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar U.S. advertising-expenditure forecasts by channel; agency fee-revenue and client-retention cohorts; platform data on advertiser self-service adoption; and a longitudinal survey of outsourced-versus-in-house media buying by advertiser size.
d5.rationale: Internet advertising revenue reached $258.6 billion in 2024, up 14.9% year over year; market research analysts and marketing specialists are projected to grow 7% over 2024–34, and advertising, promotions, and marketing managers 6%. The evidence supports continuing commercial activity but does not establish a real-volume forecast for independent media buyers, so the base is only modestly above current demand.

caveats: The NAICS definition identifies activity, not EBITDA, ownership independence, customer concentration, or transferability.
Marketplace data omit many privately negotiated sales and cannot establish eligibility for all firms.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level ownership and revenue-concentration data from PitchBook, Capital IQ, or Axial; CIMs from media-agency brokers; and interviews with transaction advisors on buyer exclusions for small agencies.
e.rationale: The code definition is directly aligned with outsourced media buying, so many LMM firms should meet the service criterion. Eligibility is reduced for firms that are effectively founder-dependent, tied to a single client or platform, lack transferable operating controls, or are establishments inside networks rather than stand-alone control-transfer targets.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541830 ($2,332,376,359) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541830 ($9,013,654,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $435,127 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541830); class <5: 406 firms, avg revenue/firm $751,810 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $117,658 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 90 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,826,767 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $598,889 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,081,677 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,264,782 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,907,357 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,428,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $94,779,679 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,833,020 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,892,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,413,115 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 121

caveats: The cited programmatic figures are from a 2019 ANA report and its surveyed advertisers are not a census of LMM customers.
No source directly measures the five-year operator-required share for the frozen lens.
o.change_evidence: Advertiser surveys tracking in-house media buying, DSP and platform self-service penetration, agency client-retention cohorts, and contract data on accountability for brand safety, measurement, and platform execution.
o.rationale: The core defined service is purchasing media for clients, and fee-based agencies remain responsible for outcomes, transparency, and contract performance. That supports persistent accountable-operator demand. Offsetting this, ANA reports programmatic use by 91% of respondents and says 35% use another third party or handle some programmatic buying in-house, showing that a material portion can shift to platforms or client teams.

caveats: ANA survey respondents are marketer members and may not represent LMM agencies.
The sources describe compensation practices, not the discounted fraction of an AI-generated gross benefit retained by an operator.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of actual media-agency MSAs, fee schedules, renewal amendments, and client procurement interviews showing whether productivity gains change fees, staffing commitments, commission economics, or scope.
q.rationale: Fee-based, labor-based, retainer, and fixed-output structures let an operator retain some workflow savings, especially before renewal. However, clients can demand lower fees or more output at renewal, and media-buying contracts are scrutinized for transparency: 69% of ANA respondents had updated contracts within three years and 60% said rebates or discounts were returned to marketers. Programmatic and digital commissions also create different pass-through incentives. These facts support partial, not full, retention.

caveats: No source measures implemented AI labor savings for the frozen six-digit lens.
Implementation rates will differ sharply between programmatic specialists and relationship-led traditional-media buyers.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after staffing and throughput data from comparable agencies; advertiser contract permissions for AI-assisted work; platform audit requirements; and client interviews on review, privacy, and brand-safety constraints.
rho.rationale: Within five years, agencies can implement AI in reporting, audience research, campaign setup support, reconciliation, and internal knowledge work, but implementation is constrained by client approvals, platform-specific controls, measurement quality, data access, privacy, brand safety, and the need to retain accountable buyers. The range is deliberately below full technical exposure.

s5.bridge: S8 measures age among all U.S. employer-firm primary owners and S9 measures voluntarily reported small-business marketplace activity across sectors, not qualifying control transfers of 541830 LMM firms. I translate the broad owner-retirement pipeline and observed active small-business transaction market into a conservative five-year probability; the proxy may overstate transfers for founder-dependent agencies and understate them for brokered professional-services firms.
caveats: S8 uses a nonrandom convenience sample that is weighted to small employer firms and is not industry-specific.
S9's transactions are voluntarily reported and do not distinguish control transfers, LMM EBITDA, or the NAICS code.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed six-digit media-agency transactions from GF Data, PitchBook, or S&P Capital IQ; SBA loan data coded to this NAICS; owner-age and succession-intent microdata for advertising-services firms; and broker interviews with completed versus failed agency sale processes.
s5.rationale: In the Federal Reserve employer-firm sample, 33% of primary owners are 55–64 and 22% are 65 or older, indicating a meaningful succession pipeline but not an intent-to-sell rate. BizBuySell reports closed transactions submitted voluntarily by brokers across 65 industries, confirming an active but selective small-business sale channel. Agency transferability, client concentration, and platform dependence justify a modest base probability rather than equating older ownership with sale.

principal_driver: Repeatable analytical, operational, and administrative media-buying workflows create a bounded but material opportunity for AI-assisted labor reduction.
principal_weakness: Platform self-service and client procurement can both reduce outsourced demand and pass agency productivity gains back to customers.
